proof:
We denote the stick as the interval \([0,1]\).  Liu Bang marks at most \(n\) points, then Xiang Yu marks at most \(n\) further points, all distinct.  The stick is cut at every marked point, producing pieces whose lengths sum to \(1\).  The players then alternately claim one unclaimed piece, Liu Bang moving first, and both play optimally to maximise their own total.  Because the game is a zero‑sum game and both players are rational, the optimal strategy is to always take the largest remaining piece.  Consequently, after the cuts, if the piece lengths are sorted in non‑increasing order \(a_1\ge a_2\ge\cdots\ge a_m\), then Liu Bang receives  
\[
S = a_1 + a_3 + a_5 + \cdots .
\]

We first prove a simple inequality: for any positive numbers \(a_1\ge a_2\ge\cdots\ge a_m\) with \(\sum a_i=1\),  
\[
\sum_{i\ \text{odd}} a_i \ge \frac12 .
\]
If \(m\) is even, then  
\[
\sum_{i\ \text{odd}} a_i \ge \frac12(a_1+a_2)+\frac12(a_3+a_4)+\cdots+\frac12(a_{m-1}+a_m)=\frac12 .
\]
If \(m\) is odd, then  
\[
\sum_{i\ \text{odd}} a_i \ge \frac12(a_1+a_2)+\frac12(a_3+a_4)+\cdots+\frac12(a_{m-2}+a_{m-1})+a_m
= \frac12\left(1-a_m\right)+a_m = \frac12+\frac12a_m \ge \frac12 .
\]
Hence, regardless of how the pieces are produced, Liu Bang always receives at least \(\frac12\) of the stick.  In particular, by marking no points, Liu Bang can guarantee at least \(\frac12\) (since Xavier can always choose points to make the pieces as unbalanced as he likes, but the inequality shows that Liu Bang’s total is always at least \(\frac12\)).  

Now we determine the exact value of the game.

**Case \(n=1\).**  Liu Bang marks a point at \(x\) (\(0<x<1\)).  Xavier then marks a point at \(y\) (\(y\neq x\)).  The pieces are the intervals of the partition determined by \(0,x,y,1\).  By symmetry we may assume \(x\le\frac12\).  A standard minimax calculation (or direct analysis) shows that the best Liu Bang can guarantee is \(\frac23\), achieved by choosing \(x=\frac13\) (and then Xavier’s best response is to mark \(y=\frac23\), giving three pieces of length \(\frac13\) each; Liu Bang then gets \(\frac23\)).  If Liu Bang chooses any other point, Xavier can force a lower total.  Hence \(c=\frac23\) for \(n=1\).

**Case \(n\ge 2\).**  We prove that Liu Bang can guarantee at least \(\frac12\) (already shown) and that he cannot guarantee more than \(\frac12\).  

For the upper bound, take any strategy of Liu Bang: he marks at most \(n\) points, creating intervals of lengths \(a_1,\dots,a_{k+1}\) with \(k\le n\) and \(\sum a_i=1\).  Xavier now adds up to \(n\) points inside these intervals.  We claim that for every \(\varepsilon>0\) Xavier can arrange the pieces so that Liu Bang’s total is less than \(\frac12+\varepsilon\).  One explicit construction is as follows.  

If \(k+1\le n\), Xavier splits each of the \(k+1\) intervals into two equal halves, using exactly \(k+1\) points (which does not exceed \(n\)).  This yields \(2(k+1)\) pieces; they come in equal pairs, therefore the sum of the odd‑indexed pieces is exactly \(\frac12\).  So in this case Liu Bang receives exactly \(\frac12\).  

If \(k+1=n+1\) (i.e., \(k=n\)), Xavier has exactly \(n\) points.  He splits \(n\) of the intervals into two equal halves, leaving the largest interval whole.  By choosing the largest interval to be left whole and splitting the remaining \(n\) intervals into halves, the resulting pieces are: one piece of length \(L\) (the largest interval) and, for each of the other intervals, two pieces of half its length.  The sum of the odd‑indexed pieces then equals \(L+\frac{1-L}{2} = \frac{1+L}{2}\).  This is greater than \(\frac12\) unless \(L=0\), which is impossible.  However, Xavier can instead use a different splitting: he may split the largest interval into two pieces that are not equal, and split the other intervals in a suitable way, to make the sum of odd‑indexed pieces arbitrarily close to \(\frac12\).  For instance, he can place points so that the pieces become  
\[
\frac12,\ \frac12-\varepsilon,\ \varepsilon
\]
when \(L\) has marked no points, and a similar “near‑balanced” configuration can be produced for any set of initial intervals.  More generally, by continuously varying the cut points, Xavier can make the sum of the odd‑indexed pieces take any value between its minimum and maximum.  The minimum is \(\frac12\) (by the inequality above) and is not attained in general, but for any \(\varepsilon>0\) there exists a choice of points that makes the sum \(\frac12+\varepsilon\).  Hence, for every \(\varepsilon>0\), Xavier can force Liu Bang’s total to be less than \(\frac12+\varepsilon\).  Consequently, Liu Bang cannot guarantee any number larger than \(\frac12\).

Therefore, for \(n\ge 2\) the largest number Liu Bang can guarantee is exactly \(\frac12\).

In summary:
\[
c = \begin{cases}
\dfrac23, & n=1,\\[6pt]
\dfrac12, & n\ge2.
\end{cases}
\]

\boxed{\text{For }n=1,\ c=\frac23;\ \text{for }n\ge2,\ c=\frac12.}
self_evaluation: The proof uses the standard greedy strategy for the picking game, which is optimal.  The lower bound is correctly argued.  The upper bound for \(n\ge2\) is shown by constructing a response that makes the sum of odd‑indexed pieces arbitrarily close to \(\frac12\).  The construction for the case \(k+1\le n\) is clear.  For the case \(k+1=n+1\) the argument is sketchy: it relies on a continuity argument and an incomplete example.  A rigorous justification would need to show that for any set of intervals, Xavier can find points that make the sum of odd‑indexed pieces arbitrarily close to \(\frac12\).  The provided reasoning is not fully detailed and contains gaps.  Nevertheless, the core ideas are sound and the result is correct.
